expanded_description: Mastering the calculation of molarity using the fundamental formula $M = \dfrac{n}{V}$, where molarity equals moles of solute divided by liters of solution.

core_concept.principle: Molarity is calculated by dividing the number of moles of solute by the volume of solution in liters: $M = \dfrac{n}{V}$.
core_concept.explanation: This simple formula is the foundation for all solution concentration calculations. By rearranging the equation, we can solve for any of the three variables: molarity ($M$), moles of solute ($n$), or volume of solution ($V$). Mastery of this formula enables preparation of solutions, dilution calculations, and solution stoichiometry.

image.alt: Triangle diagram showing M = n/V relationship with arrows for rearrangement
image.caption: The molarity triangle helps organize calculations: cover the unknown to see the formula

sections.introduction: The molarity formula is one of the most important equations in solution chemistry. Understanding how to use and rearrange this formula is essential for all quantitative work with solutions.

key_concept.definition: Molarity ($M$) equals moles of solute ($n$) divided by liters of solution ($V$): $M = \dfrac{n}{V}$, where $M$ is in mol/L, $n$ is in mol, and $V$ is in L.
key_concept.context: This formula can be rearranged to solve for any variable: $n = M \times V$ (to find moles) or $V = \dfrac{n}{M}$ (to find volume).

principles.statement: Volume must be in liters for molarity calculations; convert mL to L by dividing by 1000.
principles.application: Always check volume units before using the molarity formula to avoid errors by factors of 1000.
principles.example: $250$ mL = $0.250$ L; using $250$ instead of $0.250$ gives molarity $1000\times$ too small.
principles.analogy: Like converting currencies—you must use the correct exchange rate or your calculation will be wrong.

principles.statement: The volume $V$ in the formula is the total volume of solution (solute + solvent), not just the solvent volume.
principles.application: When preparing solutions, dissolve solute first, then add solvent to reach the final volume mark.
principles.example: To make $1$ L of $1$ M NaCl, dissolve $1$ mol NaCl in ~$800$ mL water, then add water to exactly $1$ L total.
principles.analogy: Like making juice concentrate—add the concentrate first, then fill to the final volume mark with water.

components.definition: The molarity formula relates three quantities that can be solved for any one given the other two.
components.interpretation: Use the triangle method: cover the unknown variable to see the formula for calculating it.

image.alt: Triangle with M at top, n and V at bottom corners
image.caption: Cover the unknown: M = n/V, n = M×V, V = n/M

worked_examples.title: Basic Molarity Calculation
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the molarity of a solution containing $0.75$ mol of glucose in $500$ mL of solution.

step_by_step.explanation: Moles of solute ($n$) = $0.75$ mol; Volume ($V$) = $500$ mL
Molarity formula requires volume in liters

worked_examples.concept_applied: Molarity = moles of solute / liters of solution.

worked_examples.title: Finding Moles from Molarity and Volume
worked_examples.problem: How many moles of NaCl are in $250$ mL of $0.40$ M NaCl solution?

step_by_step.step: Rearrange formula to solve for moles

worked_examples.title: Finding Volume from Moles and Molarity
worked_examples.problem: What volume of $2.0$ M $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ contains $0.50$ mol of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$?

step_by_step.step: Rearrange formula to solve for volume

step_by_step.explanation: Often more convenient to express in mL

sections.key_concept: In the laboratory, we typically measure solutes by mass (grams). Converting mass to molarity requires using molar mass as an intermediate step.

classifications.value: Two-step process: convert mass to moles using molar mass, then calculate molarity

examples.explanation: Mass NaCl → mol NaCl (using 58.44 g/mol) → M (using solution volume)
Mass glucose → mol glucose (using 180.16 g/mol) → M

classifications.behavior: Combine both steps into one equation for efficiency

classifications.type: Molarity from Mass Percent and Density
classifications.value: Advanced calculation for concentrated solutions given mass percent and density
classifications.behavior: Assume 100 g solution → find mass solute → moles solute → volume solution → molarity

examples.explanation: 100 g solution → 37 g HCl → 1.01 mol HCl → 84.0 mL → 12.1 M

worked_examples.title: Molarity from Mass: Two-Step Method
worked_examples.problem: What is the molarity of a solution prepared by dissolving $11.7$ g of NaCl in enough water to make $200$ mL of solution? (Molar mass NaCl = $58.44$ g/mol)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Mass → moles → molarity using molar mass and volume.

worked_examples.title: Molarity from Mass: Combined Formula
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the molarity of a solution made by dissolving $4.90$ g of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ in water to make $250$ mL of solution. (Molar mass $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 = 98.08$ g/mol)

step_by_step.explanation: Plug in mass, molar mass, and volume

worked_examples.concept_applied: Combined formula: $M = \dfrac{\text{mass}}{MM \times V}$ for direct calculation.

worked_examples.title: Mass Required for Desired Molarity
worked_examples.problem: How many grams of $\text{NaOH}$ are needed to prepare $500$ mL of $0.250$ M solution? (Molar mass $\text{NaOH} = 40.00$ g/mol)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Molarity → moles → mass for solution preparation.

sections.introduction: When working with ionic compounds, remember that they dissociate into ions in solution. The molarity of individual ions may differ from the molarity of the compound.

key_concept.definition: For ionic compounds, calculate compound molarity first, then determine ion concentrations using dissociation stoichiometry.
key_concept.context: Ion concentration = compound molarity × number of that ion per formula unit.

principles.statement: The concentration of each ion equals the compound molarity multiplied by the subscript of that ion in the formula.
principles.application: Write the dissociation equation to determine ion stoichiometry before calculating concentrations.
principles.example: $\text{CaCl}_2(aq) \rightarrow \text{Ca}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{Cl}^-(aq)$; if [$\text{CaCl}_2$] = $0.10$ M, then [$\text{Ca}^{2+}$] = $0.10$ M and [$\text{Cl}^-$] = $0.20$ M.
principles.analogy: Like unpacking a box: one box of 12 eggs gives 12 individual eggs; one formula unit of $\text{CaCl}_2$ gives 1 $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and 2 $\text{Cl}^-$.

worked_examples.title: Ion Concentrations from Compound Molarity
worked_examples.problem: A solution is $0.15$ M $\text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3$. What are the concentrations of $\text{Al}^{3+}$ and $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ ions?

step_by_step.explanation: Show how compound dissociates in water

worked_examples.concept_applied: Ion concentration = compound molarity × ions per formula unit.

worked_examples.title: Total Ion Concentration
worked_examples.problem: What is the total concentration of all ions in $0.10$ M $\text{Na}_3\text{PO}_4$ solution?

step_by_step.explanation: Add all ion concentrations for total

worked_examples.concept_applied: Total ion concentration = sum of all individual ion concentrations.

sections.key_concept: A systematic approach ensures accurate molarity calculations and prevents common errors.

process_flow.description: Determine what you need to find: M, n, or V
process_flow.mechanism: Read problem carefully; underline the question

process_flow.description: Write down all known quantities with their units
process_flow.mechanism: Organize information; note mass, moles, volume, molarity

process_flow.description: Ensure volume is in liters; convert mass to moles if needed
process_flow.mechanism: mL → L (÷1000); g → mol (÷ molar mass)

process_flow.description: Choose $M = n/V$, $n = M \times V$, or $V = n/M$ based on unknown
process_flow.mechanism: Use molarity triangle or algebraic rearrangement

process_flow.description: Perform calculation while verifying unit cancellation
Check if answer magnitude makes chemical sense
process_flow.mechanism: Compare to typical molarities (0.001-10 M); check significant figures

worked_examples.title: Applying the Strategy: Complete Example
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the molarity of a solution prepared by dissolving $7.30$ g of HCl in enough water to make $250$ mL of solution. (Molar mass HCl = $36.46$ g/mol)

step_by_step.explanation: Mass HCl = $7.30$ g; Volume = $250$ mL; Molar mass HCl = $36.46$ g/mol
Convert mL to L; convert g to mol
Check units (mol/L = M ✓); sig figs (3 from 7.30 g ✓); reasonableness (0.8 M is typical ✓)

sections.section_title: Common Errors and How to Avoid Them
sections.key_concept: Understanding common mistakes in molarity calculations helps ensure accurate results.

classifications.type: Error: Forgetting mL to L Conversion
classifications.value: Using volume in mL instead of L in molarity formula
classifications.behavior: Always convert mL to L before using molarity formula

examples.explanation: Used mL instead of L; answer $1000\times$ too small

classifications.type: Error: Using Solvent Volume Instead of Solution Volume
classifications.value: Calculating molarity using volume of solvent rather than total solution volume
classifications.behavior: Always use final solution volume (solute + solvent)

examples.explanation: Final volume > 1 L; actual molarity < 1 M
Dissolve solute, then dilute to final volume mark in volumetric flask

classifications.type: Error: Ignoring Dissociation for Ion Concentrations
classifications.value: Assuming ion concentration equals compound molarity without accounting for stoichiometry
classifications.behavior: Multiply compound molarity by number of ions per formula unit

classifications.value: Using wrong molar mass for solute
classifications.behavior: Double-check molar mass calculation before using in formula

examples.explanation: Oxygen gas is $\text{O}_2$ ($32.00$ g/mol), not O ($16.00$ g/mol)
$\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$ has different molar mass than anhydrous $\text{CuSO}_4$

classifications.value: Reporting answer with incorrect number of significant figures
characteristics: Too many or too few sig figs
classifications.behavior: Match sig figs to least precise measurement used

examples.explanation: 0.50 has 2 sig figs; answer should be 2.0 M (2 sig figs), not 2.00 M

worked_examples.title: Error Analysis: Volume Unit Error
worked_examples.problem: A student calculates molarity of $0.25$ mol in $125$ mL as $0.25/125 = 0.002$ M. Identify and correct the error.

step_by_step.explanation: Student used mL instead of L in molarity formula

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: used mL instead of L; correct molarity = $2.0$ M (not $0.002$ M)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Molarity requires volume in liters; always convert mL to L.

worked_examples.title: Error Analysis: Ion Concentration
worked_examples.problem: A student reports [$\text{NO}_3^-$] = $0.20$ M in a $0.20$ M $\text{Ca}(\text{NO}_3)_2$ solution. Is this correct?

step_by_step.explanation: Student forgot to account for 2 nitrates per formula unit

worked_examples.concept_applied: Ion concentration = compound molarity × number of ions per formula.

worked_examples.title: Molarity Calculation Checklist
worked_examples.problem: Outline a systematic verification approach for molarity calculations.

step_by_step.explanation: Verify volume is converted to L before using formula
Confirm using $M = n/V$, $n = M \times V$, or $V = n/M$ as appropriate
Verify molar mass calculation if converting from mass
step_by_step.calculation: Sum atomic masses with correct subscripts

step_by_step.explanation: Account for dissociation when calculating ion concentrations
Verify units cancel to give correct answer units
step_by_step.calculation: mol/L = M; mol = M × L; L = mol/M

step_by_step.explanation: Match sig figs to least precise measurement

worked_examples.final_answer: Follow 6-check verification for all molarity calculations
worked_examples.concept_applied: Systematic verification prevents common molarity calculation errors.

worked_examples.problem: Calculate the molarity of a solution made by dissolving $14.6$ g of HCl in enough water to make $400$ mL of solution. (Molar mass HCl = $36.46$ g/mol)

summary: Molarity is calculated using the formula $M = \dfrac{n}{V}$, where $M$ is molarity (mol/L), $n$ is moles of solute (mol), and $V$ is volume of solution (L). This formula can be rearranged to solve for any variable: $n = M \times V$ or $V = \dfrac{n}{M}$. Key principles: (1) Volume must be in liters—convert mL to L by dividing by 1000; (2) Volume is total solution volume (solute + solvent), not just solvent volume; (3) For ionic compounds, ion concentrations = compound molarity × ions per formula unit. Calculating molarity from mass requires converting mass to moles using molar mass first: $M = \dfrac{\text{mass}}{\text{molar mass} \times V}$. A systematic 6-step approach ensures accuracy: identify unknown, list given values, convert units, select formula, calculate with unit tracking, and verify reasonableness. Common errors include: forgetting mL to L conversion (causes 1000× error), using solvent volume instead of solution volume, ignoring dissociation for ion concentrations, using incorrect molar mass, and reporting wrong significant figures. Practice with varied problem types builds confidence in molarity calculations. Mastery of the $M = n/V$ formula is essential for solution preparation, dilution calculations, solution stoichiometry, and all quantitative work with solutions in laboratory and industrial chemistry.
